ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb chop entry; chump-specific cut is not individually enumerated. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers raw sheep/lamb meat broadly; chump-cut specificity is not separately coded. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lamb chump chop as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw lamb chump chop with bone weighs approximately 150 g, yielding around 100–110 g of edible lean and fat meat after bone removal.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, chump chop, raw — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database (F06050012)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06050012 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core per_100g macro and mineral values are taken from this approved primary regional source without modification. Fatty acid fractions (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174351 (Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, raw) as the closest approved secondary source analogue; this supplementation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per HPB Singapore Foodo (F06050012) and USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174351
gi_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA FoodData Central
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lamb chump chop (raw) contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo (F06050012, reviewed, confidence 0.95) and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174351. Glycemic index is not applicable for this food as it contains negligible carbohydrates; no GI value is assigned per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for raw lamb as it is a negligible-carbohydrate food outside the scope of GI testing.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (54 mg/100 g) with favourable potassium ratio (286 mg/100 g); saturated fat is elevated at approximately 7.8 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Raw lamb chump chop is low in sodium and suitable for hypertension management when prepared without added salt; trim visible fat before cooking to limit saturated fat intake and reduce associated cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (19.6 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb chump chop contains no carbohydrate or sugar and produces no glycaemic response, making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; choose lean preparation methods and avoid high-GI marinades or glazes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 7.8 g/100 g and cholesterol 64 mg/100 g; naturally occurring trans fat approximately 0.1 g/100 g (conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, trim all visible fat before cooking, and favour grilling or baking over frying to reduce total saturated fat and cholesterol intake from lamb chump chop.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 7.8 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; arachidonic acid (omega-6) drives TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lamb chump chop is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to elevated saturated fat and pro-inflammatory omega-6 content; if consumed, trim all visible fat and pair with anti-inflammatory omega-3-rich foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb chump chop (raw) is a bone-in cut taken from the rear chump portion of a lamb carcass, situated between the loin and the leg. The raw meat displays a deep red colour with visible marbling and has both internal and external fat attached. It is sold uncooked and requires grilling, pan-frying, roasting, or braising before consumption. The texture is firm and dense when raw, becoming tender and juicy when cooked to medium. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 234 kcal, 19.6 g protein, 17.5 g fat (of which approximately 7.8 g is saturated), and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food suitable for ketogenic and low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 54 mg/100 g and potassium is 286 mg/100 g, supporting a favourable electrolyte profile. Iron content is 2.43 mg/100 g, contributing to dietary iron needs. Cholesterol is 64 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension management when prepared without added salt and with fat trimmed, but carries moderate cardiovascular concern due to saturated fat and is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis. It is halal-certified when sourced from approved abattoirs and is gluten-free and dairy-free. Common in Australian, New Zealand, British, and Middle Eastern cuisines, and used in Singaporean mamak-style lamb preparations.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lamb chump chop?
Is lamb chump chop suitable for diabetes?
How much protein in raw lamb chump chop?
What is the fat content of lamb chump chop?
Is lamb chump chop good for high blood pressure?

Can I eat lamb chump chop if I have rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06050012, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source with an exact match to the named food item. Fatty acid fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174351 as the closest approved secondary source analogue, fully documented in notes.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as lamb is a pure animal protein and fat product containing no carbohydrate by composition. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle meat product. All three confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo (F06050012). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); no GI value is assigned per Project Veritas v2 rules. Saturated_fat_g (7.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (7.2 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174351 (Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, raw) as the closest approved-source analogue; the HPB record does not separately report fatty acid fractions. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for F06050012 and were not carried over from the USDA analogue to avoid mixing source provenance for fields not confirmed by the primary source. Cultural classification as mamak is preserved from the input record; lamb chump chop is used in mamak-style lamb preparations in Singapore. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are closest defensible matches; chump-cut specificity is not individually enumerated in either ontology as of 2026.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the precise canonical English name 'Lamb chump chop, raw'. This is a Western/International food item; no mandatory non-English local-language identity requirement applies under G6 multilingual rules. Identity is unambiguous.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (234.0), protein_g (19.6), fat_g (17.5), and carbohydrate_g (0.0) are all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (F06050012, reviewed, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source. Carbohydrate_g zero is justified in notes as naturally zero for animal muscle meat. nutrient_source clearly names both HPB and USDA FDC as the evidence basis for primary and supplementary values respectively.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — naturally zero, animal product), sodium_mg (54.0), saturated_fat_g (7.8 — estimated from USDA FDC analogue FDC ID 174351, documented in notes), and sugar_g (0.0 — naturally zero) are all present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is null (not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate food per Project Veritas v2 critical GI rule), gi_category is 'not_applicable', glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0, and gi_source documents the rationale citing HPB Foodo and USDA FDC as approved sources confirming 0.0 g carbohydrate. Setting glycemic_index to null (not 0) is correct per the critical GI rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311349 (lamb chop food product) and FoodEx2 code A00LC (meat of sheep, raw) are defensible closest_parent matches. SNOMED CT is null with honest gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, how it is prepared, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and meal_context are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key type2_diabetes), and Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record F06050012 (Lamb chump chop, raw) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. Primary nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (exact match, reviewed, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174351 (approved secondary source), fully documented. gi_source now correctly references HPB and USDA FDC as approved sources. glycemic_index is null per the critical GI rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Zero macros (carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre) justified as naturally zero for animal muscle meat. Ontology uses closest defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes with honest gap notes. All three required health conditions present with specific clinical guidance. Aliases comprehensive and internally consistent. No mandatory non-English language requirement applies to this Western/International food item.
